content.problemStatement:
Alice and Bob are playing a board game.

On their turn, a player must roll NNN standard 666-sided dice, and their action will be determined by the sum of values on the top faces of the NNN dice.

On a certain turn of Alice, she rolls the dice and obtains the sequence of values A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ on the top faces.

However, Bob isn't paying attention, allowing Alice to cheat a little!

Alice can choose a die, and flip it - so the opposite face is upward.

Note that these are standard 666-sided dice, so the sum of values on opposite faces is 777. That is:


111 is opposite to 666.
222 is opposite to 555.
333 is opposite to 444.
So as to not make Bob suspicious, Alice can perform this flipping operation **at most** KKK times.

What's the **maximum** score (i.e, sum of values of top faces of the dice) she can obtain?


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and KKK — the number of dice and the number of times Alice can flip dice.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ — the initial values of the dice.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the maximum score Alice can obtain if she flips a die at most KKK times.
constraints: The sum of N over all test cases does not exceed 2·10^5.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines. The first line contains two integers N and K — the number of dice and the maximum number of flips allowed. The second line contains N integers A1 … AN, the values shown on the dice (1 ≤ Ai ≤ 6).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line with the maximum possible sum of the top faces after performing at most K flips.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For each test case we compute the positive gains (7‑2·Ai), sort them and add the largest up to K to the initial sum. The resulting totals are 8, 17, 22 and 29 respectively.

solution.approach: For a die showing value a, flipping it changes the value to 7‑a. The gain obtained by flipping is (7‑a)‑a = 7‑2a. Only dice with a positive gain (i.e., a ≤ 3) are worth flipping. Compute the gain for every die, keep only the positive gains, sort them in descending order and add the largest up to K of them to the initial sum. If there are fewer than K positive gains, flip only those; using the remaining flips would only decrease the total sum, so they are ignored.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log N) per test case because of sorting the positive gains (the total N over all test cases is ≤ 2·10^5, so this easily fits).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for storing the positive gains of a test case.

code.Python3:
import sys

def solve():
    data = list(map(int, sys.stdin.read().strip().split()))
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = next(it)
    out_lines = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = next(it); K = next(it)
        sum_val = 0
        gains = []
        for _ in range(N):
            a = next(it)
            sum_val += a
            g = 7 - 2 * a
            if g > 0:
                gains.append(g)
        gains.sort(reverse=True)
        sum_val += sum(gains[:K])
        out_lines.append(str(sum_val))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out_lines))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
